Filed in defiance of functions.php

The Manifesto.

Eight theses on why the database-backed, plugin-soaked, admin-bar-having, auto-updating, REST-API-leaking content management system has reached the end of its useful life — and why one prompt is now enough.

  1. The CMS was a workaround for not being able to write HTML.

    That excuse no longer holds. The model can write HTML. The model can write your HTML. The CMS was scaffolding for a building that has since been printed.

  2. The plugin economy was a tax on not knowing JavaScript.

    You paid $49/year so a contact form could send an email. The replacement is a 12-line fetch, written for you, on demand, for free.

  3. "Themes" were CSS rentals.

    A theme was a stylesheet you were not allowed to read, sold to you by someone who did not want you to learn what a stylesheet was. The illusion is over.

  4. The block editor was the CMS apologizing.

    Gutenberg was an admission that nobody actually wanted to write HTML inside a textarea wrapped in three iframes. The honest version was always: just generate the HTML.

  5. Auto-updates were a confession.

    If your software needs to silently rewrite itself every two weeks to remain non-exploitable, perhaps the problem is not that users forget to click "update."

  6. "Headless" was the funeral procession.

    The moment the industry started bolting React frontends onto WordPress backends, it was telling on itself. The backend was the part nobody wanted.

  7. Static is, and always was, the answer.

    Most websites are read 10,000 times for every time they are written to. We just spent two decades pretending otherwise so we could sell hosting.

  8. The new admin panel is a sentence.

    "Make me a five-page site about X." That is the entire interface. The build step is a conversation. The deploy target is a folder.

The CMS is dead.
The prompt is the platform.

Compare it to the old way →
